attack_chain.description: Attackers gained access to Sisense's self-managed GitLab code repository via an unknown means.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the breach appears to have started when the attackers somehow gained access to the company's Gitlab code repository

attack_chain.technique_name: Data from Information Repositories: Code Repositories
attack_chain.description: Attackers accessed the self-hosted GitLab repository contents searching for sensitive data.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: in that repository was a token or credential that gave the bad guys access to Sisense's Amazon S3 buckets in the cloud

attack_chain.description: Attackers harvested an AWS token/credential that had been committed into the GitLab repository.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: in that repository was a token or credential that gave the bad guys access to Sisense's Amazon S3 buckets

attack_chain.technique_name: Use Alternate Authentication Material: Application Access Token
attack_chain.description: Attackers used the stolen AWS token to authenticate to Sisense's Amazon S3 buckets.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: a token or credential that gave the bad guys access to Sisense's Amazon S3 buckets in the cloud

attack_chain.description: Attackers accessed and copied several terabytes of customer data stored in Amazon S3, including access tokens, email passwords, and SSL certificates.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the attackers used the S3 access to copy and exfiltrate several terabytes worth of Sisense customer data, which apparently included millions of access tokens, email account passwords, and even SSL certificates

evidence_chunks.quote: the attackers used the S3 access to copy and exfiltrate several terabytes worth of Sisense customer data, which apparently included millions of access tokens, email account passwords, and even SSL certificates

the attackers used the S3 access to copy and exfiltrate several terabytes worth of Sisense customer data, which apparently included millions of access tokens, email account passwords, and even SSL certificates

attack_chain.description: Attackers exfiltrated the multi-terabyte dataset from S3 to external infrastructure, later found on a restricted access server.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: certain Sisense company information may have been made available on what we have been advised is a restricted access server

evidence_chunks.quote: certain Sisense company information may have been made available on what we have been advised is a restricted access server
certain Sisense company information may have been made available on what we have been advised is a restricted access server

initial_access_vector: Attackers gained access to Sisense's self-hosted GitLab code repository, where an AWS token/credential had been committed, providing access to Sisense's Amazon S3 buckets.
persistence_mechanism: None explicitly described; long-lived AWS credential stored in GitLab provided ongoing access.
privilege_escalation: None explicitly described; the stolen token already held privileges sufficient to read S3 buckets.
